text: The camera focuses on the direct defect of the right-sided inguinal hernia in the sixty-one-year-old male patient.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneal flap laterally, encountering some adhesion at Bogros' space, requiring adjustments and reorientation to continue the dissection smoothly.

The surgeon approaches the deep internal ring, pulling down and away to reveal the anti-sac of the direct inguinal hernia, visible as a white lining. The surgeon attempts to use scissors to peel the tissue away, but they are not functioning effectively.

The surgeon manipulates the epigastric vessels upwards and to the right, revealing the direct inguinal hernia. Muscle fibers are visibly splayed out as the surgeon sweeps the tissue up and away, using cautery to safely separate and push the hernia sac away.
The surgeon dissects inferiorly below Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle, extending the dissection down to the obturator fossa while working on the lateral plane.

The surgeon performs a high ligation, carefully peeling the hernia sac down off the vas deferens, which is visible to the right of the scissors. The camera angle is oblique, showing the tissue planes opening up with applied traction and counter traction.
The surgeon identifies two bridging veins of the coronamortis, located just above the obturator fossa.
The surgeon performs medial dissection to ensure thorough exploration, ruling out the presence of an indirect inguinal hernia. The surgeon pulls up on the tissue while searching for a cord lipoma.
The surgeon continues dissection around the corner, encountering a significant amount of adipose tissue over the iliac vessels.
The obturator nerve is visible as it is gently pulled out from the surrounding tissue in the surgical field.

The surgeon places a Bard 3 Max mesh over the direct hernia defect, preparing for high lateral fixation. The mesh is positioned medially to cover the defect, with fixation points planned at Cooper's ligament, the pubic tubercle, and the high medial portion.
The surgeon closes the flap, completing the coverage over the surgical area.
